mappings.rationaleAI: The Mali Empire (c. 1200-1600) controlled trans-Saharan gold trade routes, with Mansa Musa's 1324 Hajj demonstrating its extraordinary wealth and Timbuktu serving as a major cultural and commercial centre, directly covering the Keita dynasty, western Sudan empires, and the character of West African civilizations.

The Almoravids originated among Saharan Berber peoples of the western Maghreb and their eleventh-century conquests reshaped the Ghana Empire's political landscape, while their control of trans-Saharan trade routes connects to the development of West African monarchies and the commercial networks of the Dyula and Mande-speaking peoples.

The Atlantic slave trade across three centuries of British imperial history, including the development of slave trade routes, the African merchant class, and the abolitionist campaigns that transformed the Guinea coastlands, maps directly to the rise of the slave trade (C.2) and European antislavery movements (C.4.a).

The 1884-1885 Berlin Conference established rules for European colonial claims in Africa including free trade zones along the Niger River, directly accelerating the partition of West Africa and the imposition of arbitrary borders that marks the terminal date of this section.

British imperial expansion encompassing military conquest, colonial governance structures, and administrative systems in Africa provides essential context for the establishment of British colonial settlements at Sierra Leone, the Gold Coast, Lagos, and Ashanti from c. 1800.
